title: Southern iced tea
subtitle: Strong brewed black tea sweetened with a sugar syrup and poured over ice.
excerpt: Black tea brewed strong and hot, then sweetened with a sugar syrup and cooled. Poured over ice with lemon. The American South version, which is sweet enough to register. Served by the glass or the pitcher.
sourceNotes: Sweet iced tea is the defining non-alcoholic drink of the American South, where it is served as a default table drink in the same way water is in Europe. The practice of making cold tea sweetened with sugar became common across the Southern United States from the late nineteenth century, when ice became commercially available. The British form of iced tea typically contains less sugar than the American original, where it is frequently described as tasting like liquid candy.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a litre at a time and keep in the fridge for three days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the tea base up to three days ahead and refrigerate. Pour over ice to serve.

content.text: Southern iced tea is made from strong brewed tea sweetened while hot, which allows the sugar to dissolve fully. Adding sugar to cold tea produces a gritty residue at the bottom. The sugar syrup approach here dissolves completely and stays stable in the fridge.
Brew the tea stronger than you would for drinking hot: use more bags and a shorter steep. The ice dilutes the tea as it melts, so it needs to start concentrated.

items.prepNote: black tea, English Breakfast or similar

content.text: A heatproof measuring jug and a small saucepan for the syrup.
Put {{tea-bag}} teabags in a large heatproof jug. Pour over {{water}} of just-boiled water. Steep for 4 minutes, then remove the teabags. Do not squeeze the bags or the tea turns bitter.
Make the sugar syrup and sweeten

While the tea brews, dissolve {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in 80 ml of water in a small saucepan over a medium heat. Stir until clear, about 2 minutes. Add the hot sugar syrup to the hot tea and stir to combine. Leave to cool to room temperature, about 30 minutes, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Fill tall glasses with ice. Pour the chilled tea over the ice and add a slice of lemon. Add mint if using. The tea will look lighter as the ice begins to melt — serve promptly.

items.cause: Steeped too long or teabags were squeezed.
items.fix: Remove teabags at 4 minutes and do not press or squeeze them. Tannins in the bag are what cause bitterness.

items.cause: The 80 g sugar quantity is a moderate sweetness rather than authentically Southern.
items.fix: Add more sugar syrup. The proper Southern version would use 150–200 g of sugar per litre, which is very sweet by British standards.

items.symptom: Tea tastes watery after the ice melts
items.cause: Tea was not brewed strongly enough.
items.fix: Use six teabags for 900 ml. The dilution from ice is significant and the tea needs to start concentrated.

items.cause: Tannins precipitate when tea cools quickly.
items.fix: This is normal and does not affect flavour. Strain through a fine sieve before refrigerating if you want a clear result.

content.text: Sweet iced tea is the signature drink of the American South in the same way that tea is the signature drink of Britain: a default, expected, and regional marker. The Southern food writer Nathalie Dupree has described it as the 'house wine of the South'. In Britain, iced tea arrived mainly through American fast food chains and, later, through ready-to-drink bottles in supermarkets. The homemade version is cleaner and adjustable, and produces a drink that the bottled version does not quite replicate.
